SOUNDNESS_CORRECTNESS.justification: The paper reports strong empirical evidence (perplexity gains on PTB and FBIS and BLEU improvements on multiple NIST test sets) and compares against appropriate baselines (KN5, FFNN-LM, RNN, LSTM, genCNN, PBSMT, RNNsearch). However, the summary lacks mention of statistical significance, details ensuring fair capacity/training comparisons to baselines, and no analysis of the generated phrase forest quality versus supervised parsers—raising concerns about uncontrolled confounds. Mathematical components appear implementation-oriented (RNN pyramid and attention) with no complex proofs required, so no obvious derivation errors are reported, but the experimental support is incomplete for fully validating the large claimed gains.

ORIGINALITY.justification: The paper introduces a notable and clearly differentiated idea—phrasal RNNs that build fixed-length representations for all candidate phrases via an RNN pyramid and use a soft-attention mechanism to combine them—without relying on human-labeled syntactic data. This is a non-trivial extension beyond standard token-level RNN/LSTM and attention models (e.g., RNNsearch and genCNN), and the P/A/S subnetworks concretely separate phrase construction, attention, and sequential integration. The approach of inducing latent phrase structures end-to-end and showing large perplexity and MT improvements would likely inform experts, though it builds on existing RNN/attention components rather than proposing a wholly unprecedented paradigm.

APPROPRIATENESS.justification: The paper addresses core NLP/ML topics (neural language modeling and neural MT architectures) and proposes a novel pRNN architecture (RNN-pyramid phrase representations with attention) that is directly relevant to the community. It evaluates on standard benchmarks (PTB, FBIS, LDC corpora, multiple NIST MT test sets) against appropriate strong baselines (LSTM, genCNN, Moses, RNNsearch) using accepted metrics (perplexity and BLEU), meeting venue expectations for evaluation. Given the strong empirical claims (large PPL improvements and MT gains) and thorough related work coverage, the work is well positioned for a top-tier NLP/ML conference audience.

IMPACT.justification: The paper tackles the important problem of incorporating phrasal/hierarchical structure into neural language models without human-labeled data, proposing pRNNs that represent phrases via an RNN pyramid and attention. The reported >10-point perplexity gains over state-of-the-art LSTMs on PTB and FBIS, plus consistent MT improvements when used as an encoder (beating Moses and a strong RNNsearch baseline), suggest results that would change how researchers think about leveraging unsupervised phrase structure. The approach opens clear new directions for unsupervised phrase representation and structured RNNs and is likely to be built upon by others, though questions remain about the qualitative comparison to supervised parses.

SUBSTANCE.justification: The paper evaluates pRNNs on multiple language modeling corpora (PTB, FBIS) and a comprehensive Chinese→English MT setup with many NIST test sets, and compares against strong baselines (LSTM, genCNN, Moses, RNNsearch), indicating broadly comprehensive experiments. Hyperparameters are reported and related work coverage is described as thorough. However, the summary does not mention ablation or sensitivity analyses of the P/A/S components or the RNN pyramid, and only one limitation (unclear phrase-forest quality vs. supervised parsers) is discussed; code availability is not stated, which weakens reproducibility. These gaps keep the evaluation from being fully exhaustive.

CLARITY.justification: The paper appears well-organized and states its main contributions early, clearly describing the pRNN architecture (P, A, S subnetworks) and strong empirical claims (10+ point perplexity gains on PTB and FBIS, MT improvements on multiple NIST test sets). Notation and methodological description seem consistent and well-defined from the summary, and related work and hyperparameters are thoroughly reported. Minor presentation gaps remain—figures/tables/examples are not detailed in the summary and the open question about phrase-forest quality suggests some aspects could be clarified—hence a rating of 4.

MEANINGFUL_COMPARISON.justification: The paper compares against a comprehensive set of strong and recent baselines for both language modeling (KN5, FFNN-LM, RNN, LSTM, genCNN) and machine translation (Moses PBSMT, RNNsearch encoder–decoder). Comparisons use the same datasets and standard metrics (PTB, FBIS, LDC/NIST corpora; perplexity and case-insensitive 4-gram NIST BLEU), and the summary notes thorough related-work coverage and that the authors acknowledge limitations/open questions about phrase-forest quality. Together these facts indicate comprehensive and fair comparisons with relevant prior work.

main_claims: Introduces phrasal recurrent neural networks (pRNNs) that consider all potential candidate phrases and do not require human labeled data or external resources to construct structures.
pRNNs outperform state-of-the-art LSTM language models, achieving over 10 points improvement in perplexity on both the Penn Treebank (PTB) and FBIS English datasets.

Applying pRNNs to machine translation (as an encoder in addition to a bi-directional encoder) improves over Moses (PBSMT) and a strong RNNsearch (Encoder-Decoder) baseline on Chinese→English translation.

Proposes representing phrases as fixed-length vectors via an RNN pyramid (shifted parallel RNN sequences) and using a soft attention mechanism to selectively combine candidate-phrase representations for prediction.
_summary.methodology: pRNN comprises three subnetworks—P (constructs fixed-length phrase representations from word embeddings), A (soft attention that weights candidate phrase representations given the current hidden state), and S (sequential RNN that incorporates the weighted phrase summary into the next hidden state). The model builds an RNN pyramid of shifted parallel RNN sequences to represent phrases and uses attention over candidate phrases when predicting the next word.
datasets: LDC corpora (1.25M sentence pairs for MT training)
limitations: Unclear how the quality of the phrase forest generated by pRNN compares to supervised parsing models trained on human-labeled data; the authors identify this as an open question.
_summary.topic_area: NLP - language modeling and machine translation
